nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving blackwater streams and flooded forest tributaries of the upper Rio Negro and upper Orinoco basins; tannin-stained water with very soft, highly acidic conditions, sandy substrate, leaf litter, and dense riparian vegetation

diet.notes: Micro omnivore; tends to accept small prepared foods including crumbled flakes, nano/micro pellets, and freeze-dried tubifex. Live and frozen micro foods — baby brine shrimp, cyclops, daphnia, and bloodworm — are generally relished and recommended for maintaining condition and colour.

avoidWith: any fish large enough to swallow them

summary: The smallest of the three Paracheirodon tetras, recognized by its vivid iridescent green-blue stripe and subdued red. Generally considered advanced due to strict blackwater chemistry requirements; mostly wild-caught.
careNotes:
The Green Neon Tetra is the smallest of the three Paracheirodon species and tends to be the most demanding in terms of water chemistry. It generally does best in mature, biologically established aquariums with stable, soft, acidic water closely resembling its blackwater origins. Water parameter swings are reported to be particularly stressful for this species, especially for wild-caught specimens, which account for the vast majority of trade stock — captive-bred individuals are rarely available commercially.

Water chemistry is critical: pH is generally advised in the 4.0–6.5 range, with hardness ideally at or near zero (gH 0–4, kH 0–2). This is notably stricter than both the Neon Tetra (Paracheirodon innesi) and Cardinal Tetra (Paracheirodon axelrodi), which tolerate somewhat harder and more neutral water. Some sources note that captive-acclimated specimens can be maintained successfully at higher pH (up to 7.0–7.5) once fully settled, but this is not recommended for initial acclimatisation of wild stock.

The species is noticeably smaller and paler than its congeners. The brilliant blue-green iridescent stripe runs the length of the body; the red stripe is restricted to the lower caudal region and is considerably duller than in the Cardinal Tetra or Neon Tetra. All three species (P. simulans, P. innesi, P. axelrodi) constitute the entire genus Paracheirodon.

A school of at least 8 individuals is recommended; 10–15 or more in larger setups is preferable. Dim lighting, dark substrate, driftwood, and soft leaf litter will help them display their best colouration and reduce stress. Gentle filtration is important — the species is native to slow-flowing waters and does not tolerate strong currents well.

FishBase (post-reclassification) lists the family as Acestrorhamphidae; most aquarium hobby sources and SeriouslyFish retain the traditional Characidae placement.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been documented but is generally considered difficult and is rarely achieved in the hobby. Commercial breeding at scale is essentially absent — virtually all trade specimens are wild-caught from the Rio Negro and adjacent blackwater tributaries. Breeding appears to require very soft, acidic water (pH around 5.0–6.0, near-zero hardness), dim or blackout conditions, and has been reported to occur nocturnally. Eggs are thought to be light-sensitive. Spawning in schools rather than isolated pairs has been noted to improve success rates. Fry require infusoria and micro foods at first feeding.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary source; adult size, temperature (74–82°F / 23–28°C), pH (3.0–6.5 natural; up to 7.0–8.0 in captivity), tank size (10 gal minimum), swim zone, diet, temperament, grouping, and native range. Tank size recommendation biased toward 60 L per playbook guidance for proper schooling group of 8+.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Characidae/Characiformes), size (15–25 mm), pH (3.0–6.5), hardness (18–143 ppm ≈ 1–8 dGH; lower end strongly preferred), tank dimensions (45×30 cm base ≈ 60 L equivalent), gregarious schooling behaviour. Temperature listed as 21–35°C including natural river extremes; aquarium range narrowed to 23–28°C per other sources. Wild-caught sensitivity and breeding rarity confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (2.0 cm SL), temperature (23–27°C), pH (5.5–6.0), hardness (up to 4 dH), native range (upper Negro and Orinoco basins). FishBase classifies family as Acestrorhamphidae; Characidae retained per hobby consensus. pH range averaged across all sources to 4.0–6.5 for aquarium recommendation.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (~2.5 cm), temperature (75–85°F / 24–29°C), pH (5.0–6.5), native range (Orinoco and Negro Rivers), lifespan (2–3 years captivity), and minimum group size (6–8). Lifespan averaged with cardinal tetra data to give 2–5 year range. Venezuela confirmed in native range.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity; virtually all trade stock is wild-caught. Requires soft acidic blackwater conditions, dim/blackout lighting, and nocturnal spawning environment. Fry need infusoria and micro foods.
